summary: Luu provides a detailed critique of Julia's engineering culture and practices, arguing that despite the language's impressive technical features and performance claims, it suffers from systematic quality issues stemming from inadequate testing, poor error handling, and a core team culture that dismisses legitimate concerns. The post catalogs numerous bugs encountered in basic usage, examines structural problems in the development process (lack of code review, poor documentation, weak test coverage), and critiques misleading performance benchmarks. A 2022 update demonstrates that many core issues persist years later despite claims they were fixed, with the community's reflexive defensiveness and PR-focused responses preventing meaningful improvement in correctness and reliability.
key_points: Julia has an unusually high rate of serious correctness bugs in both the language runtime and core libraries, with silent wrong results being common
The development culture prioritizes velocity over correctness but publicly claims world-class reliability and testing rigor
Performance benchmarks on the Julia website are unrepresentative microbenchmarks that avoid Julia's performance pitfalls while not giving other languages similar advantages
High barriers to contribution (broken builds, recursive make issues, undocumented code even core devs can't understand) limit community participation
Core team's pattern of dismissing legitimate criticism as 'illegitimate' or claiming issues are 'already fixed' continues for years while the same problems persist
money_quotes: I spent half an hour writing a throwaway script and ran into four bugs in the core language.
Julia folks don't care about error handling, so it's buggy and incomplete.

It's hard to say that a community is friendly when the response is mostly positive, but with a significant fraction of nasty responses, since it doesn't really take a lot of nastiness to make a group seem unfriendly.

There's a sleight of hand here where the reflexive response from a number of the co-creators as well as core developers of Julia is to brush off any particular issue with a comment that sounds plausible if read on HN or Twitter by someone who doesn't know people who've used Julia.
Julia and its packages have the highest rate of serious correctness bugs of any programming system I've used, and I started programming with Visual Basic 6 in the mid-2000s.
key_insight: Julia's core problem isn't technical limitations but a development culture that systematically undervalues correctness, testing, and documentation while publicly claiming excellence in these areas, creating a gap between marketed reliability and actual user experience that persists because legitimate criticism is reflexively dismissed rather than addressed.
